front: When was the Battle of Hastings?
back: 1066 — William the Conqueror defeated King Harold II

front: What is the Domesday Book?
back: A survey of land and property in England ordered by William I in 1086

front: What was the Magna Carta?
back: A charter of rights signed by King John in 1215, limiting the power of the king

front: When did the Black Death reach England?
back: 1348 — killed about one-third of the population

front: What were the Wars of the Roses?
back: Civil wars (1455–1485) between Lancaster (red rose) and York (white rose) for the English throne

front: Who won the Battle of Bosworth Field (1485)?
back: Henry Tudor (Henry VII), defeating Richard III and starting the Tudor dynasty

front: Why did Henry VIII break with Rome?
back: The Pope refused to annul his marriage to Catherine of Aragon; he established the Church of England

front: Name Henry VIII's six wives in order
back: Catherine of Aragon, Anne Boleyn, Jane Seymour, Anne of Cleves, Catherine Howard, Catherine Parr

front: Mnemonic for Henry VIII's wives' fates?
back: Divorced, Beheaded, Died, Divorced, Beheaded, Survived

front: When was the Spanish Armada defeated?
back: 1588 — during the reign of Elizabeth I

front: What was the Gunpowder Plot?
back: A failed plot in 1605 by Guy Fawkes and others to blow up the Houses of Parliament and kill James I

front: What was the English Civil War about?
back: Conflict (1642–1651) between Parliament and King Charles I over governance and religion

front: Who was Oliver Cromwell?
back: Led parliamentary forces in the Civil War; became Lord Protector of the Commonwealth (1653–1658)

front: What was the Glorious Revolution?
back: In 1688, William of Orange invaded England and replaced James II without significant bloodshed

front: What was the Bill of Rights (1689)?
back: An Act of Parliament that limited the power of the monarchy and guaranteed Parliament's rights

front: What did the Act of Union 1707 do?
back: United the kingdoms of England and Scotland, creating the Kingdom of Great Britain

front: When did the slave trade end in the British Empire?
back: The Emancipation Act of 1833 abolished slavery throughout the British Empire

front: What was the significance of the Great Reform Act of 1832?
back: Extended voting rights to more men and made the electoral system more representative

front: When did women first get the vote in the UK?
back: 1918 — women over 30 could vote. Full equal voting rights came in 1928 (women over 21)

front: What was VE Day?
back: 8 May 1945 — Victory in Europe Day, marking the end of WWII in Europe

front: What was the significance of the SS Empire Windrush?
back: In 1948, it brought the first large group of Caribbean migrants to the UK, marking the start of post-war immigration

front: When was the NHS established?
back: 1948 — providing free healthcare to all UK residents

front: Who were the Suffragettes?
back: Women who campaigned for the right to vote. Key figure: Emmeline Pankhurst

front: What was Hadrian's Wall?
back: A wall built by the Romans around AD 122 to keep out the Picts (northern tribes) in what is now northern England

front: When did the Romans leave Britain?
back: AD 410 — to defend other parts of the Roman Empire

front: Who was the first Norman King of England?
back: William I (William the Conqueror) — crowned on Christmas Day 1066

front: What happened at the Battle of Agincourt?
back: In 1415, Henry V led a famous English victory against the French during the Hundred Years War

front: Who was Elizabeth I?
back: The last Tudor monarch (1558–1603), known as the Virgin Queen. Defeated the Spanish Armada, oversaw a cultural golden age

front: What was the Restoration?
back: In 1660, Charles II restored the monarchy after the Commonwealth period under Cromwell

front: Who built Stonehenge and when?
back: Built in several stages between 3000 BC and 1500 BC. We don't know exactly who built it

front: How many MPs are in the House of Commons?
back: 650 — each representing a constituency

front: What is the House of Lords?
back: The upper house of Parliament. Members are appointed, not elected. It reviews and suggests changes to laws

front: What is the role of the Prime Minister?
back: Leader of the political party with the most seats in the House of Commons; heads the government

front: What is the Cabinet?
back: A group of senior ministers chosen by the PM, each responsible for a government department

front: Who is the Head of State in the UK?
back: The Monarch (currently King Charles III)

front: What is a constitutional monarchy?
back: A system where the monarch is Head of State but their powers are limited by law; the government is run by elected officials

front: What is a general election?
back: An election for all 650 seats in the House of Commons. Must be held at least every 5 years

front: Who can vote in UK general elections?
back: UK citizens, Irish citizens, and qualifying Commonwealth citizens aged 18+ who are registered to vote

front: What is devolution?
back: The transfer of some powers from central government to Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland

front: What is the role of the Speaker?
back: The Speaker chairs debates in the House of Commons and ensures rules are followed. They are politically neutral

front: What are the main political parties in the UK?
back: Conservative, Labour, Liberal Democrats, Scottish National Party (SNP), Plaid Cymru, Green Party

front: What is the minimum voting age in the UK?
back: 18 years old (16 in Scotland and Wales for devolved elections)

front: What is the role of local government?
back: Provides local services including education, housing, planning, roads, and waste collection

front: What are the fundamental principles of British law?
back: Everyone is equal before the law; laws are made in Parliament; laws can be changed through democratic process

front: What is the European Convention on Human Rights?
back: An international treaty that protects human rights and fundamental freedoms. The UK was instrumental in drafting it

front: What is the role of the judiciary?
back: Interprets and applies the law. Judges are independent of the government

front: What is a by-election?
back: An election held in a single constituency when the sitting MP dies, resigns, or is disqualified

front: What is the electoral register?
back: The list of people registered to vote. You must be on the register to vote in elections

front: What is the Commonwealth?
back: A voluntary association of 56 countries, mostly former territories of the British Empire

front: What does 'first past the post' mean?
back: The voting system used in UK general elections. The candidate with the most votes in each constituency wins

front: What is Hansard?
back: The official written record of everything said in Parliament

front: What is a referendum?
back: A vote in which the entire electorate is asked to give an opinion on a particular question, like the 2016 EU referendum

front: What is the role of the Opposition?
back: The second-largest party in the Commons. The Leader of the Opposition leads an alternative government (Shadow Cabinet)

front: Who is the Chancellor of the Exchequer?
back: The government minister responsible for financial and economic policy, including the Budget

front: What is a constituency?
back: A geographical area represented by one MP in the House of Commons. The UK has 650 constituencies

front: What are the patron saints of each UK country?
back: England: St George (23 Apr), Scotland: St Andrew (30 Nov), Wales: St David (1 Mar), N. Ireland: St Patrick (17 Mar)

front: What is Bonfire Night?
back: 5 November — commemorates the failed Gunpowder Plot of 1605 with fireworks and bonfires

front: What is Remembrance Day?
back: 11 November — commemorates those who died in wars, especially WWI and WWII. People wear red poppies

front: What are UK bank holidays?
back: Official public holidays: New Year, Easter, Early May, Spring, Summer Bank Holidays, Christmas Day, Boxing Day

front: What are the national flowers of the UK?
back: England: Rose, Scotland: Thistle, Wales: Daffodil, N. Ireland: Shamrock

front: Who wrote Romeo and Juliet?
back: William Shakespeare — born in Stratford-upon-Avon in 1564, considered the greatest English playwright

front: Name 4 plays by Shakespeare
back: Hamlet, Macbeth, A Midsummer Night's Dream, Romeo and Juliet (also: The Tempest, Othello, King Lear)

front: Who wrote Pride and Prejudice?
back: Jane Austen — one of the most famous English novelists, lived 1775–1817

front: Who painted The Fighting Temeraire?
back: JMW Turner — one of the most celebrated British painters

front: What is the Proms?
back: Annual BBC-hosted classical music concerts at the Royal Albert Hall. The Last Night is a famous celebration

front: What sport was invented in England?
back: Cricket — also, association football (soccer), rugby, golf (Scotland), and tennis

front: What is Hogmanay?
back: Scottish New Year's Eve celebration, with traditions like 'first footing'

front: What is the Edinburgh Festival?
back: An annual arts festival in August, including the Fringe — the largest arts festival in the world

front: What is the National Trust?
back: A charity that preserves and protects historic places and green spaces in England, Wales, and N. Ireland

front: Who was Charles Dickens?
back: Victorian novelist — wrote A Christmas Carol, Oliver Twist, Great Expectations, David Copperfield

front: What is the Eisteddfod?
back: A Welsh cultural festival celebrating poetry, music, and performance. Held annually

front: Who was Robert Burns?
back: Scotland's national poet. Wrote Auld Lang Syne. Burns Night is celebrated on 25 January

front: What is the Turner Prize?
back: An annual contemporary art prize named after JMW Turner, awarded at Tate Britain

front: Who designed the Crystal Palace?
back: Joseph Paxton — built for the Great Exhibition of 1851 in Hyde Park, London

front: What is Mothering Sunday?
back: The fourth Sunday of Lent — the UK version of Mother's Day

front: Who was Florence Nightingale?
back: Pioneer of modern nursing who improved hospital conditions during the Crimean War (1853–1856)

front: What are the Six Nations?
back: An annual rugby union championship between England, Scotland, Wales, Ireland, France, and Italy

front: What is Boxing Day?
back: 26 December — a UK bank holiday, traditionally a day for giving gifts to servants and the poor

front: What is the Brit Awards?
back: The main British music awards ceremony, equivalent to the US Grammys

front: Who are The Beatles?
back: A hugely influential rock band from Liverpool (1960s). Members: John Lennon, Paul McCartney, George Harrison, Ringo Starr

front: What are the fundamental values of British life?
back: Democracy, rule of law, individual liberty, tolerance, and mutual respect

front: What is the rule of law?
back: Everyone is subject to the law, including the government. No one is above the law

front: What does 'individual liberty' mean in UK values?
back: The right to hold your own opinions, beliefs, and to live your life freely within the law

front: What is the Human Rights Act 1998?
back: Incorporates the European Convention on Human Rights into UK law. Protects rights like free speech, privacy, and fair trial

front: What does the Equality Act 2010 protect against?
back: Discrimination based on age, disability, gender, race, religion, sex, sexual orientation, marriage, pregnancy

front: What is Freedom of Speech in the UK?
back: The right to express opinions freely, but subject to laws against incitement to hatred, defamation, and threats

front: Is the UK a secular state?
back: The UK has an established church (Church of England), but people are free to practise any religion or none

front: What is the minimum age for marriage in England?
back: 18 years old (16–17 with parental consent was abolished in 2023 in England and Wales)

front: What are the responsibilities of a UK resident?
back: Obey the law, respect others' rights, pay taxes, serve on a jury if called, vote in elections

front: What is volunteering in the UK?
back: An important tradition. About 25% of people volunteer regularly. It is not compulsory but is encouraged

front: What is the Magna Carta's significance today?
back: It established the principle that the king is subject to the law and laid the foundation for constitutional governance

front: What age can you buy alcohol in the UK?
back: 18 years old. You can drink at home from age 5 (with parental permission)

front: What is the age of criminal responsibility in England?
back: 10 years old

front: What is antisocial behaviour?
back: Behaviour that causes harassment, alarm, or distress. Can include noise, vandalism, and intimidation

front: What rights do EU citizens have in the UK after Brexit?
back: Those who applied to the EU Settlement Scheme by 30 June 2021 can continue to live and work in the UK

front: What is zero tolerance of domestic violence?
back: Domestic violence is a crime in the UK. Anyone experiencing it should report to police or call helplines

front: What is forced marriage in UK law?
back: Forced marriage is illegal in the UK. Both parties must freely consent to marriage

front: What is FGM in UK law?
back: Female Genital Mutilation is a serious criminal offence in the UK, even if performed abroad

front: What is the driving age in the UK?
back: 17 years old for a car. 16 for a moped

front: What does 'mutual respect and tolerance' mean?
back: Respecting others' views, beliefs, and lifestyles even if they differ from your own

front: What side of the road do you drive on in the UK?
back: The left side

front: What is the emergency services number in the UK?
back: 999 (also 112 from a mobile phone)

front: What is the NHS?
back: National Health Service — provides free healthcare to all UK residents. Founded in 1948

front: How do you register with a GP?
back: Visit your local GP surgery and ask to register. You will need proof of address and ID

front: What is National Insurance?
back: A tax on earnings that funds benefits, state pension, and the NHS. You need a NI number to work

front: What is Council Tax?
back: A local tax based on property value that pays for local services like rubbish collection, police, and fire services

front: What is a TV licence?
back: Required to watch live TV or use BBC iPlayer. Funds the BBC

front: At what age must children attend school in England?
back: 5 to 18 years old (can do an apprenticeship or training from 16)

front: What are GCSEs?
back: General Certificate of Secondary Education — exams taken at age 15–16 in England, Wales, and N. Ireland

front: What are A levels?
back: Advanced level qualifications taken at 17–18. Often required for university entry

front: What is UCAS?
back: Universities and Colleges Admissions Service — handles applications to higher education

front: What is the MOT test?
back: An annual safety and emissions test required for vehicles over 3 years old

front: What documents do you need to drive in the UK?
back: A valid driving licence, vehicle insurance, MOT certificate (if car is 3+ years old), and vehicle tax

front: What is the currency of the UK?
back: Pound sterling (GBP / £). 100 pence = 1 pound

front: What is a National Insurance number?
back: A unique personal number used for tax and benefits. Format: 2 letters, 6 digits, 1 letter (e.g., QQ 12 34 56 C)

front: Where can you get legal advice for free?
back: Citizens Advice Bureau, Community Legal Service, legal aid (if eligible)

front: What number do you call for non-emergency police?
What is a tenancy agreement?
back: A legal contract between a landlord and tenant that sets out the terms of renting a property

front: What is the State Pension age in the UK?
back: 66 years old (rising to 67 by 2028)

front: What is self-assessment?
back: A system where self-employed people report their income and pay tax directly to HMRC

front: What is Childline?
back: A free counselling service for children up to age 19, run by NSPCC. Call 0800 1111

front: What is jury service?
back: UK residents aged 18–75 can be called to serve on a jury. It is a civic duty and you must attend unless excused

front: How can you become a UK citizen?
back: Apply for naturalisation — must have lived in UK for 5 years (3 if married to a UK citizen), pass Life in the UK test, and meet English language requirements

front: What is the Life in the UK test?
back: A computer-based test with 24 questions about British history, values, and traditions. You need 75% to pass

front: What is Jobcentre Plus?
back: A government agency that helps people find employment and claim benefits

front: Who was Boudicca?
back: Queen of the Iceni tribe who led a revolt against the Romans around AD 60–61

front: What were the Crusades?
back: Religious wars in the Middle Ages. Richard the Lionheart fought in the Third Crusade (1189–1192)

front: What was the Peasants' Revolt?
back: In 1381, peasants led by Wat Tyler marched on London to protest against a poll tax

front: What was the Great Fire of London?
back: In 1666, a fire destroyed much of central London. It started in a bakery in Pudding Lane

front: Who was Isaac Newton?
back: Born 1643. Discovered the laws of gravity and motion. One of the greatest scientists in history

front: What was the Industrial Revolution?
back: Period from mid-18th century when Britain led the world in manufacturing, powered by steam engines and factories

front: Who was Isambard Kingdom Brunel?
back: A great Victorian engineer who designed bridges (Clifton Suspension), tunnels, and the Great Western Railway

front: What was the Battle of Trafalgar?
back: In 1805, Admiral Nelson defeated the French and Spanish fleets. Nelson died in the battle

front: What was the Battle of Britain?
back: In 1940, the RAF defended Britain against the German Luftwaffe, preventing a German invasion

front: Who was Winston Churchill?
back: Prime Minister during WWII (1940–1945, 1951–1955). Famous for inspiring speeches and wartime leadership

front: What is the Notting Hill Carnival?
back: An annual Caribbean-inspired street festival in London, held on August Bank Holiday weekend. The largest in Europe

front: What is Wimbledon?
back: The oldest tennis tournament in the world, held annually in London since 1877. Known for its grass courts

front: What is the FA Cup?
back: The oldest football cup competition in the world, open to all English football clubs

front: Who wrote A Christmas Carol?
back: Charles Dickens — published in 1843, featuring the character Ebenezer Scrooge

front: What is the Globe Theatre?
back: A theatre in London associated with William Shakespeare. A reconstruction was built in 1997 near the original site

front: When did the Romans first invade Britain?
back: 55 BC — Julius Caesar led the first Roman expedition to Britain

front: When was Hadrian's Wall built?
back: 122 AD — Built by Emperor Hadrian to keep out the Picts, stretching 73 miles coast to coast

front: Who led a revolt against the Romans around AD 61?
back: Boudicca — Queen of the Iceni tribe in eastern England

front: What was the Domesday Book?
back: A great survey of England commissioned by William the Conqueror in 1086 to assess land and wealth

front: When did the Great Fire of London occur?
back: 1666 — Destroyed much of the City of London; led to rebuilding in stone including St Paul's Cathedral

front: What was the Blitz?
back: German bombing campaign against British cities 1940-1941 during WWII. London was bombed for 57 consecutive nights.

front: Who was Robert the Bruce?
back: King of Scots who defeated the English at the Battle of Bannockburn (1314) and secured Scottish independence

front: What was the Easter Rising?
back: 1916 — An armed rebellion in Dublin against British rule, a key event leading to Irish independence

front: What was the Good Friday Agreement?
back: 1998 peace agreement that largely ended The Troubles in Northern Ireland. Established power-sharing government.

front: Who was Isambard Kingdom Brunel?
back: Victorian engineer who built the Great Western Railway, Clifton Suspension Bridge, and SS Great Britain

front: Where does the Scottish Parliament meet?
back: Holyrood, Edinburgh. The Scottish Parliament has powers over education, health, and law.

front: What is the Welsh Senedd?
back: The Welsh Parliament (formerly Welsh Assembly), based in Cardiff. Has powers over education, health, and transport in Wales.

front: How does a Bill become an Act of Parliament?
back: A Bill passes through readings and debates in both Commons and Lords, then receives Royal Assent to become law.

front: What is the role of the Speaker?
back: The Speaker chairs debates in the House of Commons, maintains order, and is politically impartial.

front: What is a by-election?
back: An election held between general elections when a seat in Parliament becomes vacant (e.g., due to death or resignation of an MP).

front: What is the electoral register?
back: The official list of people eligible to vote. You must be on it to vote in elections. Registration is a legal requirement.

front: How many countries are in the Commonwealth?
back: 56 countries. The King is Head of the Commonwealth. Members cooperate on trade, development and democracy.

front: What is the role of the Chancellor of the Exchequer?
back: Responsible for economic and financial matters. Presents the annual Budget to Parliament.

front: What does 'first past the post' mean?
back: The UK's voting system where the candidate with the most votes in each constituency wins the seat.

front: What is the Northern Ireland Assembly?
back: The devolved legislature at Stormont, Belfast. Operates on a power-sharing basis between unionist and nationalist parties.

front: When is Bonfire Night and what does it celebrate?
back: 5 November — Celebrates the failure of the Gunpowder Plot (1605) when Guy Fawkes tried to blow up Parliament.

front: When is Remembrance Day?
back: 11 November — Honours those who died in wars. A two-minute silence at 11am. People wear red poppies.

front: What is Hogmanay?
back: Scottish New Year's Eve celebration on 31 December. Traditions include first-footing and singing Auld Lang Syne.

front: What is the Edinburgh Festival Fringe?
back: The world's largest arts festival, held annually in Edinburgh in August. Features theatre, comedy, and music.

front: What is the Notting Hill Carnival?
back: Annual Caribbean carnival held in London on August bank holiday weekend. Largest street festival in Europe.

front: What is the Eisteddfod?
back: A Welsh festival of literature, music, and performance. The National Eisteddfod is held in Welsh.

front: Where is Wimbledon and what sport is it famous for?
back: Wimbledon in London hosts the oldest and most prestigious tennis tournament in the world (since 1877).

front: What is The Ashes in cricket?
back: A test cricket series between England and Australia. Named after the symbolic 'ashes' of English cricket.

front: Who wrote Oliver Twist and Great Expectations?
back: Charles Dickens — One of Britain's greatest novelists, writing about Victorian social conditions.

front: What is the Turner Prize?
back: A prestigious annual contemporary art award, named after the painter JMW Turner.

front: What is the Human Rights Act 1998?
back: Incorporates the European Convention on Human Rights into UK law. Protects rights like freedom of expression and fair trial.

front: What is the Equality Act 2010?
back: Protects people from discrimination based on 9 protected characteristics including age, race, sex, and disability.

front: What are the fundamental British values?
back: Democracy, the rule of law, individual liberty, and mutual respect and tolerance of different faiths and beliefs.

front: At what age can you vote in UK general elections?
back: 18 years old. The voting age was lowered from 21 to 18 in 1969.

front: What is jury service?
back: A civic duty where citizens help decide court cases. Most UK residents aged 18-75 on the electoral register can be called.

front: What did the suffragettes campaign for?
back: The right for women to vote. Led by Emmeline Pankhurst. Women over 30 got the vote in 1918; all women over 21 in 1928.

front: What is the minimum age for marriage in the UK?
back: 18 years old (raised from 16 in England and Wales).

front: When was same-sex marriage legalised?
back: 2014 in England, Wales and Scotland. Civil partnerships have been available since 2004.

front: What is the age of criminal responsibility in England?
back: 10 years old — below this age, children cannot be charged with a criminal offence.

front: What is the Rule of Law?
back: The principle that no one is above the law, including the government. Laws apply equally to everyone.

front: What number do you call in an emergency?
back: 999 (or 112). For non-emergency medical help, call 111. For non-emergency police, call 101.

front: What is the approximate population of the UK?
back: About 67 million people.

front: What are the four countries of the UK and their capitals?
back: England (London), Scotland (Edinburgh), Wales (Cardiff), Northern Ireland (Belfast).

front: What side of the road do you drive on in the UK?
back: The left side. The UK is one of the few countries that drives on the left.

front: What ages is primary school in England?
back: Ages 5 to 11 (Reception to Year 6).

front: What are GCSEs?
back: General Certificate of Secondary Education — exams taken at age 15-16 in England, Wales and Northern Ireland.

front: How do you register with a GP?
back: Visit your local GP surgery and complete a registration form. You can find your nearest surgery on the NHS website.

front: What is the current national minimum wage age?
back: Workers aged 23+ receive the National Living Wage. Lower rates apply for younger workers and apprentices.

front: What is council tax?
back: A tax paid to your local authority for local services like rubbish collection, police, and fire services. Based on property value.

front: What is a National Insurance number?
back: A unique personal number used for tax and benefits. Format: two letters, six numbers, one letter (e.g., AB 12 34 56 C).
